C:
The single most important move is to stop treating "signup → paid" as one number. Plenty of signups with few conversions almost always means the leak is *activation* — people sign up and never reach the moment your product proves its worth — not pricing. But you don't actually know that yet, so the first work is diagnostic, not a redesign.

Here's how I'd approach it.

**1. Instrument the funnel before changing anything.** Break the journey into stages: signup → setup complete → first value action → habit (repeated use) → paid. Define one concrete "activation moment" — the action that, historically, correlates with people who *did* pay (e.g. "invited a teammate," "connected their data source," "created 3 projects"). Then measure what fraction of signups reach each stage. The cliff usually announces itself.

**2. Read the cliff — it forks into two very different problems.**
- *Most signups never hit the activation moment* → this is an onboarding/time-to-value problem. The fix is reducing friction to first value: guided setup, sample/templated data so an empty product isn't staring at them, removing steps that require IT or integration work up front, and a "what's the one thing to do next" nudge.
- *Many activate but still don't pay* → this is a monetization or buying-process problem, not a product one. Likely causes: the free tier is generous enough to never need an upgrade, there's no moment where a limit actually bites, or the person who got value isn't the person who can buy.

**3. B2B-specific levers, because the user ≠ the buyer.** Capture company and role at signup so you can separate qualified accounts from individual tire-kickers — your "low conversion" may partly be denominator pollution. For high-fit accounts (right company size, multiple signups from the same domain), add a sales-assist motion rather than relying on pure self-serve. And design the expansion path: individual gets value → invites the team → an economic buyer enters → procurement. Paywalling collaboration/seats tends to convert better in B2B than paywalling core features, because the value compounds with the team.

**4. Consider a reverse trial.** Give full functionality for 14 days, then drop to a limited free tier. This lets people reach the activation moment with no friction, and the downgrade creates the natural "I miss that" pressure that a permanently-generous free tier never does.

**5. Run it as a test loop, not a redesign.** Pick the single biggest cliff, form one hypothesis, change one thing for one segment, and measure conversion of that cohort. Resist the urge to fix five things at once — you won't know what worked.

If I had to pick where to start: **instrument the funnel and find the activation moment first.** Everything downstream depends on knowing whether you have an activation problem or a monetization problem, and those have nearly opposite fixes. Spending a week on pricing experiments when the real issue is that 80% of signups never finish setup would be wasted motion.

One thing I'd want to know to sharpen this: is your motion currently self-serve (credit card, no sales) or sales-assisted, and roughly what company size are you selling into? That changes which of the above matters most.

**Where the funnel leaks most — my bet, to confirm in step 1.** The single biggest bottleneck in B2B self-serve is almost always the very first stage: **signup → setup complete (the first session in an empty product).** This is where the largest share of signups drops out — typically more than activation, habit, and the paywall combined — because the product asks for setup or integration work before it has shown any value. So focus there first. The data from step 1 might overturn this (if most signups actually finish setup and the drop is later, at the paywall, then it's a monetization problem instead) — but if you have to act before the instrumentation lands, act on the first-session cliff.

**The #1 test to run first:** ship a guided first-run with templated/sample data that gets a new user to the activation moment inside their first session, with no integration required up front. Run it on new signups only as a 50/50 split against current onboarding, and measure activation rate (fraction reaching the activation moment) of each cohort, then their downstream paid conversion. That's the one experiment to launch.

**Hold for later** — explicitly do *not* run these in parallel with the first test: pricing/packaging changes, the reverse trial, and the sales-assist motion. Each addresses a *downstream* cliff (monetization or buying process), and running them now would both muddy attribution and waste effort if, as expected, the leak is upstream at first-session setup. Sequence them only after the activation cliff is fixed and step 1 confirms where the next-largest drop sits.

If I had to pick where to start: **fix the signup → setup-complete cliff — the biggest single leak — via the guided-setup test above.** Everything downstream depends on knowing whether you have an activation problem or a monetization problem, and those have nearly opposite fixes. Spending a week on pricing experiments when the real issue is that 80% of signups never finish setup would be wasted motion.

One thing I'd want to know to sharpen this: is your motion currently self-serve (credit card, no sales) or sales-assisted, and roughly what company size are you selling into? That changes which of the above matters most.
P:
The single most important move is to stop treating "signup → paid" as one number. Lots of signups but few conversions almost always points to an *activation* leak — people sign up and never reach the moment your product proves its worth — not a pricing problem. But that's a hypothesis, not a finding, so the first week is diagnosis, not redesign.

**1. Diagnose the funnel two ways at once — quantitative and qualitative.**

*Quantitative:* break the journey into stages — signup → setup complete → first value action → repeat use → paid — and measure the drop-off at each. Define the "activation moment" empirically, not by gut: pull your accounts that *did* convert, look at what they did in their first session or week that non-converters didn't, and you'll usually find one or two actions that separate them (invited a teammate, connected a data source, created their third project). That behavior is your activation metric. The biggest cliff in the stage-by-stage numbers tells you where the money is leaking.

*Qualitative:* in parallel, get on calls with 8–10 people — a few recent converters, a few who signed up and went cold. Ask what they were trying to do, what they expected, and what stopped them. Funnel data tells you *where* people drop; interviews tell you *why*, and they do it in a day instead of a month of A/B tests. Do both; neither alone is enough.

**2. Read the cliff — it forks into two problems with nearly opposite fixes.**

- *Most signups never hit the activation moment* → an onboarding / time-to-value problem. Reduce friction to first value: guided setup, sample or templated data so people don't face an empty product, removing steps that need IT or integration work up front, and a clear "do this one thing next" nudge.
- *Many activate but still don't pay* → a monetization or buying problem, not a product one. Usual causes: a free tier generous enough that the limit never bites, no moment where hitting that limit actually hurts, the person who got value can't authorize spend, or — easy to miss — a clumsy last mile (no self-serve checkout, confusing seat math, "contact sales" where a credit card should be). Check the path from *wanting* to pay to *having* paid; it leaks more often than people expect.

**3. B2B-specific levers, because the user isn't the buyer.** Capture company and role at signup so you can split qualified accounts from individual tire-kickers — part of your "low conversion" may just be denominator pollution, and the real rate among fit accounts is healthier than the blended number suggests. For high-fit signups (right company size, multiple people from one domain), add a sales-assist motion instead of relying on pure self-serve. And design the expansion path deliberately: individual gets value → invites the team → an economic buyer enters → procurement. Paywalling collaboration and seats tends to convert better in B2B than paywalling core features, because the value compounds as the team grows.

**4. Consider a reverse trial.** Full functionality for 14 days, then drop to a limited free tier. People reach the activation moment with zero friction, and the downgrade creates the "I miss that" pressure a permanently generous free tier never produces.

**5. Run it as a test loop, not a redesign.** Take the single biggest cliff, write one hypothesis, change one thing for one segment, and measure that cohort's conversion. Resist fixing five things at once — you won't know what worked, and some fixes cancel each other out.

If I had to pick where to start: **instrument the funnel and talk to ten users — find the activation moment before touching anything else.** Whether you have an activation problem or a monetization problem decides everything downstream, and the fixes point in opposite directions. A week of pricing experiments is wasted motion if the real issue is that 80% of signups never finish setup.

Two things would sharpen this, and they change the emphasis rather than the approach: whether your motion is self-serve (credit card, no sales) or sales-assisted, and roughly the company size and deal size you're selling into. Small ACV and self-serve push everything toward frictionless activation and the free-tier limit; larger ACV makes the sales-assist motion and the buyer-vs-user gap the main event. Tell me which you are and I'll tailor it.
